finding: A selection policy favoring an underrepresented group recruited sufficient high-performing individuals to preserve overall task efficiency.
A cognitive processing mode (analytic) causally promoted disbelief in a specific domain (religious belief).
Recent execution of a cognitive process (encoding/retrieval) biased subsequent performance on a related cognitive task.
A state of resource scarcity altered attention allocation, which in turn predicted self-reinforcing behavioral choices.
A bodily cue conveyed emotional polarity that facial features alone did not, and contextually reversed perceived affect.
A state of economic scarcity reduced cognitive capacity independent of objective resource availability or physiological stress.
Exposure to a specific narrative genre improved performance on social-cognitive judgment tasks.
Absence of external sensory stimulation produced an aversive internal state that motivated acceptance of a noxious stimulus.
A policy that decoupled a donor's payment from operational costs increased donation rate and total donations substantially.
Infants extracted and generalized abstract rule structures from limited linguistic input.
The framing of decision options reversed preference ordering even when objective outcomes were identical.
Static analysis error patterns were consistent across student subpopulations and showed no relation to eventual program correctness in the near term.
A perception that an item was AI-generated reduced trust, but only when contrasted with alternate human-generated items.
Depletion of a cognitive resource (mental fatigue) reduced physical performance through increased perception of effort, not through physiological mechanisms.
People use both nominal and real value representations, producing a systematic bias toward nominal evaluation.
Individual differences in cognitive control predicted adoption of a computational strategy in a separate decision-making domain.
A gender difference in selection into a competitive environment arose from overconfidence and preference differences, not performance differences.
A folk concept (intentional action) judgment was influenced by moral evaluation, whereas a related concept (intention) was not.
Folk intuitions about a metaphysical property (free will) shifted between contexts, with context determining the folk ontology applied.
A capability signal reduced hiring preference because it signaled lower organizational commitment and higher flight risk.
